data.title: Broadcom, Honeywell, Zscaler
data.description:
Wall Street securities analysts revised their ratings and price targets on several U.S.-listed companies, including Broadcom, Honeywell and Zscaler on Friday.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • * Broadcom Inc : HSBC raises target price to $240 from $225

  • * Cloudflare : Keybanc initiates coverage with overweight rating; price target $210

  • * Honeywell : Jefferies raises target price to $235 from $215

  • * Zscaler Inc : Canaccord Genuity raises target price to $320 from $305

Following is a summary of research actions on U.S. companies reported by Reuters on Friday. Stock entries are in alphabetical order.

data.title: Nomura Warns of Higher Deflation Risks in Thailand, Expects Policy Rate Cuts
data.description:
Nomura kept its 2025 GDP growth forecast unchanged for Thailand at 1.8%, warning of a sharp slowdown in the second half and the risk of a technical recession, according to its latest Asia Economic Monthly report published Friday.

The Japanese research firm, however, cut its headline inflation forecast to -0.3% for both 2025 and 2026, citing higher deflationary pressures, weakening domestic demand, and tight financial conditions.

Nomura expects the Bank of Thailand to cut its policy rate by an additional 75 basis points this year to 1%, with the next cut likely at the June 25 meeting.

On the fiscal front, the deficit is expected to widen to 5.6% of GDP in FY2025, with stimulus totaling around 500 billion Thai bahr, or 2.7% of GDP, the report added.

data.title: European Industry Contracts as Tariffs Pull Exports Lower — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl and Joshua Kirby

Industrial production in Europe's two largest nations declined in the first month of President Trump's global tariff blitz, with goods exports to the U.S. from Germany also falling sharply.

German industrial output contracted 1.4% on month in April, statistics agency Destatis said Friday, a sharper fall than the 1.0% decline expected by economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.

That offsets much of the 2.3% jump in output in March, when U.S. firms stockpiled imports to get ahead of the impact from expected tariffs.

Trade data, also published Friday, showed goods exports to the U.S. sinking 10.5% as companies adapted to the new post-"Liberation Day" trade reality. Overall, exports from Germany fell 1.7%.

Production had been improving at the start of 2025, having posted the largest quarterly increase in the first three months of the year since the start of 2022. That was ahead of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine that sent energy prices higher and for years curbed the competitiveness of Germany's energy-intensive industry.

In France, the eurozone's second-largest economy, production also declined 1.4%, quashing the signs of recovery booked there in THE previous two months, and flipping consensus expectations that output would rise.

April's data suggests that just as U.S. stockpiling boosted some overseas economies in the first quarter, the end of that process is set to be a headwind for them in this quarter.

Production dived 18% for the export-orientated pharmaceutical industry in Germany after a strong rise in March, with machinery production also feeding the overall decline, Destatis said.

Manufacturers can take comfort from the European Central Bank's rate cut on Thursday, which could free up the ability to borrow more. The new German government, led by Friedrich Merz, has announced plans to massively boost spending on defense and infrastructure that could increase demand for industrial goods toward the end of this year.

ECB President Christine Lagarde flagged the opposing trends at the central bank's rate-decision meeting.

"While the uncertainty surrounding trade policies is expected to weigh on business investment and exports, especially in the short term, rising government investment in defense and infrastructure will increasingly support growth over the medium term," she said.

However, the data suggests the boost to industrial output early this year has already reversed, and that underlying activity is still weak, Capital Economics economist Franziska Palmas said in a note to clients.

"With U.S. tariffs likely to continue to weigh on industrial output and the outlook for demand for German industrial goods poor, we think production will remain subdued this year," she said.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com and Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com

data.title: Fiserv to Acquire Remaining 49.9% Stake in Irish Joint Venture
data.description:
Fiserv said Friday it agreed to acquire the remaining 49.9% stake in its Irish joint venture, AIB Merchant Services.

Financial terms of the deal weren't disclosed.

The transaction requires approval from regulators and is expected to complete in Q3.

Fiserv formed AIB Merchant Services, a payments firm, in 2007 with AIB Group.

data.title: China Stocks Struggle Despite Trump-Xi Call
data.description:
The Shanghai Composite edged up 0.04% to close at 3,385, while the Shenzhen Component slipped 0.19% to 10,183 on Friday, as mainland Chinese stocks struggled for clear direction following a high-level call between US President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping.

The two leaders agreed to continue trade talks, easing concerns around the latest dispute over rare earths.

However, the absence of concrete details kept investors cautious, with expectations that heightened tensions between them will remain a persistent market overhang.

Attention is now shifting to China’s upcoming inflation and trade data, scheduled for release on Monday, which may offer deeper insight into the country’s economic trajectory.

Among individual movers, Eoptolink Tech (+2.9%) and Avic Chengdu (+3.9%) posted strong gains, while Shenzhen Forms (-7.6%) and Contemporary Amperex (-1.0%) weighed on the broader market.

For the week, the Shanghai Composite rose 1.13% and the Shenzhen Component gained 1.42%.

data.title: King Long Motor's Bus Production, Sales Decline in May
data.description:
Xiamen King Long Motor's bus production fell 2.4% to 4,082 units in May from 4,183 in the year-ago period, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Bus sales plunged nearly 22% to 3,575 units from 4,554 units in the prior-year period.

Shares closed 3% lower during Friday's afternoon trading.

data.title: India's IndusInd Bank rises as RBI deputy says things should settle soon
data.description: Shares of India's IndusInd Bank rose 3% on Friday after a deputy governor at the country's central bank said "things should soon settle down" at the lender, which has grappled with accounting lapses.

data.title: Thailand inflation remains negative in May; government cuts 2025 forecast
data.description:
Thailand's annual inflation rate was negative for a second straight month in May, the commerce ministry said on Friday as it cut its full-year forecast to close to zero, just weeks ahead of the central bank's next monetary policy review.

The consumer price index (THCPI=ECI) dropped 0.57% in May from a year earlier, not quite as steep as the 0.80% fall forecast in a Reuters poll, following a 0.22% fall in April.

It was the third month in a row that the inflation rate has been below the central bank's target range of 1.0% to 3.0%.

Poonpong Naiyanapakorn, head of the ministry's Trade Policy and Strategy Office, said a positive inflation reading was expected in June, but the forecast of 0.2% to 0.4% was still below the central bank's target.

The ministry cut its full-year inflation forecast to 0.0% to 1.0% from 0.3% to 1.3% seen previously.

Poonpong said prices fell in May due to lower energy prices and more production of agricultural goods such as vegetables.

The core CPI (THCPIX=ECI), which excludes volatile food and energy prices, was up 1.09% in May from a year earlier, higher than a forecast rise of 0.94%.

For the first five months of the year, headline inflation averaged 0.48% and the core rate was at 0.95%, Poonpong said.

In April, the Bank of Thailand cut its key interest rate by a quarter point to 1.75%, the lowest level in two years, and reduced its growth and inflation forecasts for 2025, due to risks from U.S. tariffs. The next rate meeting is on June 25.

Deputy BOT Governor Piti Disyatat told Reuters last month the central bank was ready to ease policy again if needed to support Southeast Asia's second-largest economy through the global trade war. He said low inflation was not a reflection of weak domestic demand or deflation.

data.title: Kioxia Targets AI-Driven SSD Growth, Aims for Mid-20% Operating Margin
data.description:
Flash memory maker Kioxia outlined its medium to long-term growth strategy on Thursday, focusing on expanding the role of SSDs in AI infrastructure and boosting profitability through product innovation and disciplined investment.

The company expects nearly half of NAND demand to be AI-related by 2029, driven by growth in inference systems and AI-capable smartphones and PCs.

To address this demand, Kioxia is developing high-performance, large capacity SSDs, including the CM9 and LC9 series built on its eighth-generation BiCS FLASH.

Production of the eighth-generation BiCS FLASH is ramping up, while development of the ninth and tenth generations is underway. Kioxia also plans to sample a super-high IOPS SSD, delivering over 10 million IOPS, in 2026.

The company aims to keep capital expenditures below 20% of revenue and maintain research and design spending at 8% to 9%. It targets an average operating margin in the mid-20% range and a long-term shift to a net cash position.

data.title: Dassault Systemes Needs to Deliver Faster Growth to Hit Target — Market Talk
data.description: Dassault Systemes extended its midterm target of doubling earnings to 2029 from 2028, and it will need to grow faster than it has over much of the past decade to meet its goal, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. The French software company's target implies a compound annual growth rate of 15% over the 2025-29 period, which compares with growth of about 9% it achieved in the better part of the past decade, the analysts say. "Given Dassault's track record, most will assume back-end loaded targets leave risk on the table," the analysts say. "The challenge for Dassault today is to explain why this midterm reset won't lead to a similar reset in two years' time." Shares fall 0.7%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: FTSE 100 Set for 4th Weekly Gain
data.description:
The FTSE 100 traded flat to slightly higher on Friday, holding near three-month highs and on track for a modest weekly gain, its fourth straight, marking the best streak since January.

Market sentiment was supported by a call between US President Trump and China’s President Xi, which Trump described as very good and mainly focused on trade.

Investors are now awaiting key US nonfarm payroll data, expected to show a monthly job decline.

On the corporate front, AstraZeneca received EU approval for its Calquence treatment for leukemia.

UK regulators banned bonuses for executives at six water companies due to pollution concerns.

HSBC's chairman announced his departure amid ongoing restructuring, while Rio Tinto’s CEO denied speculation of a board rift behind his resignation.

Meanwhile, Halifax reported a 0.4% drop in UK house prices in May, but noted the market remains broadly stable, with annual prices still up 2.5% thanks to improved mortgage rates and steady wage growth.

data.title: France Current Account Gap Largest in Over 2 Years
data.description:
France recorded a current account deficit of EUR 4.1 billion in April 2025, reversing from a EUR 1 billion surplus in the previous month.

This marked the largest current account shortfall since February 2023, as the goods account gap widened to EUR 8.9 billion from EUR 4.3 billion in March.

Additionally, the services account surplus narrowed to EUR 4.7 billion from EUR 5.3 billion in the prior month, as well as the primary income surplus to EUR 3.9 billion from EUR 4 billion.

Meanwhile, the secondary income deficit remained unchanged at EUR 3.9 billion for the third consecutive month.
